text: The surgeon enters through the right upper pararectal area using an optical port, carefully splitting each layer of the abdominal wall without cutting the muscle fibers. Upon entering the peritoneal cavity, the port is turned parallel to the abdominal wall before further insertion. The second port, the camera port, is introduced through the umbilicus, and the telescope is switched to this port for visualization.

The surgeon deflates the inflated stomach using a gastric calibration tube. The third port, a 5mm left upper pararectal port, is utilized as a right-hand working port, while the right upper pararectal port serves as a left-hand working port.

The surgeon begins devascularizing the greater curvature of the stomach starting opposite the incisura angularis using a ligature medullum as a vessel sealer. The greater omentum appears thin at this level, facilitating the process. The lesser sac is opened, and devascularization continues towards and beyond the pylorus. A bubble grasper retracts the stomach to the left, while a Sure Maryland instrument is used through the right-hand working port to perform the devascularization.

The surgeon carefully dissects towards the pylorus, avoiding the pyloric antrum to prevent thermal injury to the stomach wall. The Ligasure Maryland is used for dissection, with its insulated jaws providing protection around the first part of the duodenum. A bubble grasper through the epigastric port retracts the stomach anteriorly, allowing division of the bulky greater omentum in layers between the gastroepiploic vascular arcade and the greater curvature.

The surgeon divides the greater omentum in layers, using the lower jaw of the Ligasure Maryland to dissect deep to the anterior layer before division. The dissection extends five centimeters beyond the pylorus, below the first part of the duodenum. The first assistant retracts the stomach towards the left side using a bubble grasper through the epigastric port, allowing the remaining layers of the greater omentum to be divided.

The pyloric antrum is retracted anteriorly using a bubble grasper through the epigastric port, while the pancreatic-gastric attachments are carefully divided layer by layer. The surgeon avoids holding the pyloric antrum or the first part of the duodenum with the tip of the Ligasure Maryland. The pancreaticoduodenal peritoneal reflection is opened, revealing the course of the gastroduodenal artery. The dissection proceeds with caution to prevent injury to the duodenum.

The surgeon carefully divides loose areolar tissue from the first part of the duodenum, while branches of the gastro-duodenal artery are encountered and divided. A gauze soaked in diluted adrenaline is used to control any bleeding. The stomach is retracted anteriorly, revealing a thin peritoneal membrane of the lesser momentum over the caudate lobe, which is divided to create a window. The first part of the duodenum is mobilized, with dissection continuing to separate it from surrounding structures, including adhesions with the transverse colon, gallbladder, liver, and duodenum.

The surgeon opens the pancreatic duodenal peritoneal reflection, revealing the gastro-duodenal artery, which is partially obscured by the pancreatic head. Dissection occurs below and behind the pyloric antrum and the first part of the duodenum, as well as superior to the duodenum. The surgeon uses the Ligasure Maryland to divide branches of the gastro-duodenal artery, carefully teasing fat from the duodenum and ensuring the duodenal wall is not injured.

The surgeon replaces the bowel grasper with a liver retractor through the epigastric port to facilitate dissection above the pyloric antrum and the first part of the duodenum. A blunt-tipped instrument is used for gentle liver retraction. The thin peritoneal membrane of the lesser omentum is divided, revealing the course of the right gastric artery. Care is taken to preserve the blood supply to the first part of the duodenum from the lesser omental vascular arcade while avoiding injury to the gallbladder and common bile duct.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the origin of the right gastric artery and continues dissection below and behind the first part of the duodenum. The pyloric antrum is retracted anteriorly using a bubble grasper through the left-hand working port, facilitating access to the dissection area. The common hepatic artery is visible below the jaws of the Ligasure Maryland, and additional branches of the gastroduodenal artery are divided to prevent bleeding from the staple line.

The surgeon dissects around the origin of the right gastric artery, clearly visualizing its pulsations alongside the common hepatic artery. Using the Ligasure Maryland, the surgeon divides the origin of the right gastric artery without clips, ensuring not to injure the gallbladder or common bile duct. A branch of the gastro-duodenal artery is also divided, and the lesser omental fat is dissected to complete the duodenal mobilization. The course of the gastro-duodenal artery is now fully visible, and the right gastric artery is divided at its origin to allow free mobility of the sleeve, pyloric antrum, and first part of the duodenum.

The surgeon continues the resection upwards towards the esophageal hiatus, retracting the stomach to the right side and the liver anteriorly using a bubble grasper through the left-hand working port. The greater omentum is pushed to the left side to identify the junction between the stomach and the greater omentum. The surgeon carefully retracts the stomach and liver using the bubble grasper through the epigastric port, while retracting the omentum towards the left side. As the dissection approaches the hiatus, the surgeon stays slightly away from the stomach to prevent thermal injury and releases all pancreatic gastric attachments. The blood supply to the upper pole of the spleen is identified and preserved, noting the proximity of these vessels to the stomach wall.

The surgeon divides the pancreaticogastric peritoneal reflection, carefully avoiding deep medial dissection to prevent injury to the left gastric artery. Using a Ligasure Maryland, the upper short gastric vessels are divided while retracting the stomach and liver with a bubble grasper through the epigastric port. The pulsations of the left gastric artery are visible, and dissection continues layer by layer until the fundus is exposed, taking care not to injure the stomach wall. The dissection proceeds until the left crest of the diaphragm is identified, with precautions taken to avoid injury to the gastroesophageal junction, esophagus, and fundus of the stomach.

The surgeon uses an iDrive power gun with tri-staplers, inserting the stapler through the right upper pararectal port. The stapler is carefully positioned away from the crow's foot to prevent gastric sleeve narrowing. After firing the first stapler, a gastric calibration tube is introduced by the anesthetist and maneuvered to the medial side of the staple line. Clips are applied at the starting, junctions, and ending points of the staple line to manage any bleeding.

The surgeon removes any loose clips at the end of staple firing and applies the second stapler lateral to a point where blood vessels disappear on the stomach surface. The stapler is fired through the left-hand working port, ensuring symmetry by checking both sides of the stomach. Clips are applied at staple line junctions and bleeding points, avoiding excessive pressure to prevent interference with subsequent stapler firing. The stapler gun is used to articulate and push the sleeve medially for uniform firing in a straight line.

The surgeon fires the fourth stapler using Medtronic tri-staplers, applying clips at any bleeding points observed after stapler firing. Continuous stapler firing is employed, with an option to fire incrementally to displace tissue. The sixty mm fifth stapler is used to ensure complete removal of the gastric fundus, maintaining a gap of five millimeters to one centimeter from the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon applies two clips on the sleeve side before dividing the tissue using a vessel sealer, as the stapler did not cut completely. Ligasure Maryland is used to divide between the clips on the sleeve and specimen sides. The completed gastric sleeve is placed over the right lobe of the liver. The gastric calibration tube is removed to facilitate smooth division with a blunt-tipped instrument. A 45mm stapler is used to divide the first part of the duodenum, positioned two to six centimeters distal to the pylorus.

The surgeon uses a 45 mm ply stapler to divide the first part of the duodenum, ensuring it is positioned 2 to 6 centimeters distal to the pylorus. Care is taken to avoid injury to the gastro-duodenal artery, common hepatic artery, and common bile duct. The stapler is lifted to retract the liver, maintaining blood supply to the duodenum without discoloration. Surgicil is applied to cover the duodenal stump, with clips placed over any bleeding points on the staple line. The sleeve and duodenum are observed to be freely mobile, and the greater omentum is pushed into the suprapubic compartment. The common channel length is measured from the ileocecal junction, using two bowel graspers, with the surgeon standing on the left side of the patient.

The surgeon uses bubble graspers with printed measurements to measure the small bowel, counting in increments of five centimeters. The small bowel is gently held over the antimesenteric border and slightly stretched without overstretching. The common channel length is measured to be 350 centimeters, marked with a 3-0 glyconate suture through the bowel wall on the mesenteric side. The transverse mesocolon is pulled anteriorly using a bubble grasper introduced through the epigastric port, and the biliopancreatic limb length is measured starting at the duodenal flexure.

Two bowel graspers are introduced through the primary right and left hand working ports to measure the biliopancreatic limb length. The surgeon adjusts the limb lengths by increasing the biliopancreatic limb length to 390 centimeters and reducing the common channel length to 300 centimeters. The measurement is completed by counting towards the ileocecal junction, ensuring the orientation of the bowel is maintained to prevent twisting the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper introduced through the epigastric port to retract the end of the suture towards the right upper quadrant. The loop duodenal ileal anastomosis is performed in two continuous layers: the outer seromuscular layer and the inner layer. The first part of the duodenum is opened using a Ligasure Maryland, with the option to remove or include the duodenal staple line in the inner layer.

The surgeon opens the first part of the duodenum and applies suction to clear secretions. The ileum is then opened on the antimesenteric border using a Ligasure Maryland, followed by suction to clear secretions. A 3-0 glyconate suture is used to apply an inner continuous layer, with the needle passed from outside to inside on the ileal wall and from inside to outside on the duodenal wall, forming a knot on the outside. The suture is pulled snugly to ensure a watertight anastomosis. An atraumatic grasper through the epigastric port applies traction on the suture end. The inner continuous layer is full thickness or full thickness extramucosal, and care is taken to avoid injuring blood vessels while suturing.

The surgeon completes the inner continuous layer of the anastomosis by applying a knot at the starting point on the left side of the patient. The needle used for this layer is removed. The posterior half of the outer continuous seromuscular layer is then continued as the anterior half, moving from the left side to the right side of the patient. This additional layer is applied for extra security to prevent anastomotic leaks.

The surgeon ensures minimal tension on the anastomosis by dividing the right gastric artery, facilitating a tension-free connection between the sleeve and the first part of the duodenum. The sleeve and duodenum are mobilized into the infracolic compartment for anastomosis. The suture material is pulled snugly after each bite to approximate the edges, while maintaining tension on previous bites to prevent injury to blood vessels. The anastomosis is rotated during final bites, and a knot is applied at the right end to complete the outer continuous layer.

The final appearance of the duodenal-ileal anastomosis is shown, completed in two continuous layers without separate anchoring sutures. The gastric sleeve is covered with a single layer of Surgicel to prevent potential post-operative bleeding from the staple line.

The surgeon covers the sleeve, anastomosis, and duodenal stump with the greater omentum. The rejected stomach is placed in a sterile polythene bag and removed through the right upper para-rectal port. The right suprahepatic space is washed with saline, and the port is sealed from the inside using the Ligasure Maryland.
